info.name: sporty-jackpot
info.description:
Jackpot service — every HTTP endpoint the application exposes.

The service has exactly two endpoints:

- `POST /api/bets` — publish a bet. Returns **202 Accepted**: the bet has been accepted for asynchronous processing, but nothing has been persisted and no pool has moved yet.
- `POST /api/bets/{betId}/evaluate` — evaluate a processed bet for a jackpot win. **POST, not GET**, because a win pays out the pool and resets it.

**Run order matters.** A bet must be published *and processed* before it can be evaluated. Under the default `mock` profile processing is synchronous, so evaluate works immediately. Under the `kafka` profile the bet travels through a real broker, so evaluating too quickly returns **404** until the consumer has caught up — wait a moment and retry.

**Bet ids are generated per run.** Each publish request has a pre-request script that writes a fresh unique `betId` into a collection variable, which the paired evaluate request then reads. This is deliberate: `betId` is the idempotency key, so re-sending a bet id that has already been processed is silently skipped rather than contributing twice.

**A bet gets exactly one evaluation.** Calling evaluate a second time returns the recorded outcome without rolling again — a win replays the stored reward, a loss stays a loss.

Seeded jackpots (from `application.yml`), all starting at a pool of 1000.00:

| id | contribution | reward |
|---|---|---|
| `classic-progressive` | FIXED 5% | FIXED 10% |
| `must-drop` | FIXED 5% | VARIABLE, base 1%, 100% at pool 5000.00 |
| `fast-start` | VARIABLE 10% decaying to 2% | FIXED 10% |
| `dynamic` | VARIABLE 10% decaying to 2% | VARIABLE, base 1%, limit 5000.00 |

The H2 console is not a REST endpoint and is not included here. Browse it at http://localhost:8080/h2-console (JDBC URL `jdbc:h2:mem:jackpot`, user `sa`, empty password).

item.name: Happy path
item.description: Publish a bet, then evaluate it. The win is a genuine roll at the jackpot's configured chance, so the evaluate step may legitimately return `won: false`.

exec: // Fresh id per run: betId is the idempotency key, so reusing one
// would be skipped as a duplicate delivery instead of contributing.

request.description:
Publishes a bet to the `jackpot-bets` topic (or logs it and processes it inline under the `mock` profile).

`fast-start` uses VARIABLE contribution: the rate starts at 10% and decays linearly as the pool grows, floored at 2%. A 100.00 stake against a fresh 1000.00 pool contributes 10.00.

**Response — 202 Accepted**
```json
{ "betId": "bet-...", "status": "ACCEPTED" }
```

202 rather than 201: no resource exists yet at any location, and the pool has not moved. `status` reports that the bet was taken for processing, not that a contribution was applied.

exec: pm.test('rewardAmount is null exactly when won is false', () => {

request.description:
Evaluates the bet published above. On a win the reward is the **entire** current pool and the jackpot resets to its initial value.

**Response — 200 OK**
```json
{ "betId": "bet-...", "won": false, "rewardAmount": null }
```
```json
{ "betId": "bet-...", "won": true, "rewardAmount": 1010.00 }
```

`rewardAmount` is null exactly when `won` is false — a loss is not a payout of zero.

POST rather than GET because a win mutates server state. Repeat calls return the same stored answer, but that is not HTTP idempotence: the first call is what moves the money.

Returns **404** if the bet has not been processed yet — expected under the `kafka` profile if you evaluate immediately after publishing.

item.name: Guaranteed win — must-drop
item.description:
A deterministic win, useful for demonstrating the payout and reset without relying on chance.

`must-drop` contributes a FIXED 5% and its VARIABLE reward chance reaches 100% once the pool hits 5000.00. A single stake of 80000.00 contributes exactly 4000.00, taking the pool from 1000.00 to precisely the limit — so the following evaluation always wins.

Run these two in order. They only behave as described against a `must-drop` pool sitting at its initial 1000.00; after a win it resets there, so the pair is repeatable.

request.description: 80000.00 × 5% = 4000.00, taking the `must-drop` pool from 1000.00 to exactly its 5000.00 reward limit.

The pool is at the reward limit, so the win chance is exactly 1.0.

**Response — 200 OK**
```json
{ "betId": "bet-...", "won": true, "rewardAmount": 5000.00 }
```

Pays out the whole pool and resets `must-drop` to 1000.00. Call it again and it returns the same stored reward without rolling again and without touching the pool.

item.name: Other seeded jackpots
item.description: The two remaining contribution × reward combinations, for exercising each strategy pair.

request.description:
Flat 5% in, flat 10% chance out. A 200.00 stake contributes 10.00 regardless of pool size.

Sets `betId`, so the "Evaluate bet" request in the Happy path folder will evaluate this one.

Both sides move with the pool: the contribution rate decays from 10% toward its 2% floor as the pool grows, while the reward chance climbs from 1% toward 100% at a 5000.00 pool.

Send this repeatedly to watch the effective contribution rate fall — each response is only 202, so read the application log or the `jackpot_contribution` table to see it.

Sets `betId`, so the "Evaluate bet" request in the Happy path folder will evaluate this one.

exec: pm.test('reports every offending field at once', () => {

request.description:
Three violations in one body: blank `betId`, blank `userId`, and a zero `betAmount`.

`betAmount` is `@Positive`, not `@PositiveOrZero` — a zero bet would contribute nothing while still occupying a bet id, so it is rejected at the edge rather than becoming a no-op record.

**Response — 400 Bad Request**
```json
{
  "error": "VALIDATION_FAILED",
  "fieldErrors": {
    "betId": "must not be blank",
    "userId": "must not be blank",
    "betAmount": "must be greater than zero"
  }
}
```

Every offending field is reported at once, so the caller does not fix them one request at a time.

**Response — 404 Not Found**
```json
{ "error": "BET_NOT_FOUND", "message": "..." }
```

404 rather than a reported loss. A bet row is written only once the bet has been consumed and contributed, so an id with no bet behind it is a request about a resource that does not exist. Reporting it as a loss would be a lie the caller cannot detect, and would mask both a typo'd id and a bet still queued for processing.

This is also the response you get under the `kafka` profile if you evaluate before the consumer has processed the bet.

exec: pm.test('202 Accepted — the publish itself is fine', () => {

request.description:
The endpoint still returns **202** — it does not know which jackpots exist, and validation only checks that `jackpotId` is non-blank.

Downstream, the contribution service looks the jackpot up, finds nothing, logs a warning and returns normally without recording anything. The spec contributes only "if there is such a jackpot", so this is not an error condition; throwing would fail the Kafka listener and put a permanently unprocessable message into an endless redelivery loop.

No bet row is written, so evaluating this `betId` afterwards returns **404**. Watch the application log for:
```
Bet ... references unknown jackpot 'no-such-jackpot' — no contribution recorded
```

Deliberately uses a hard-coded `betId` rather than a generated one.

**Send it twice.** Both calls return 202, but only the first moves the pool. `betId` is the idempotency key: Kafka delivery is at-least-once, so the same bet legitimately arrives again after a restart or a rebalance, and a `betId` already present in the bet table is skipped rather than contributed twice.

The second send logs:
```
Bet fixed-duplicate-bet has already been processed — skipping duplicate delivery
```

The skip is decided on `betId` alone — change the amount and it is still skipped. The first delivery wins.

Note this only resets when the application restarts, since the database is in-memory.
